is a [town](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") located along the [Connecticut River](/wiki/Connecticut_River "Connecticut River") in [Coös County](/wiki/Co%C3%B6s_County,_New_Hampshire "Coös County, New Hampshire"), [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"), United States. The town is named after the city of [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_Lancashire "Lancaster, Lancashire") in England. As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the town population was 3,218,[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2020-3) the second largest in the county after [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire "Berlin, New Hampshire"). It is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of Coös County and gateway to the [Great North Woods Region](/wiki/Great_North_Woods_Region_(New_Hampshire) "Great North Woods Region (New Hampshire)") of the state. Lancaster, which includes the villages of Grange and South Lancaster, is home to [Weeks State Park](/wiki/Weeks_State_Park "Weeks State Park") and the Lancaster Fair. Part of the [White Mountain National Forest](/wiki/White_Mountain_National_Forest "White Mountain National Forest") is in the eastern portion. The town is part of the [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire "Berlin, New Hampshire"), NH−[VT](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") [Micropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire_micropolitan_area "Berlin, New Hampshire micropolitan area").

**Lancaster** is a [town](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") located along the [Connecticut River](/wiki/Connecticut_River "Connecticut River") in [Coös County](/wiki/Co%C3%B6s_County,_New_Hampshire "Coös County, New Hampshire"), [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"), United States. The town is named after the city of [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_Lancashire "Lancaster, Lancashire") in England. As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the town population was 3,218,[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2020-3) the second largest in the county after [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire "Berlin, New Hampshire"). It is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of Coös County and gateway to the [Great North Woods Region](/wiki/Great_North_Woods_Region_(New_Hampshire) "Great North Woods Region (New Hampshire)") of the state. Lancaster, which includes the villages of Grange and South Lancaster, is home to [Weeks State Park](/wiki/Weeks_State_Park "Weeks State Park") and the Lancaster Fair. Part of the [White Mountain National Forest](/wiki/White_Mountain_National_Forest "White Mountain National Forest") is in the eastern portion. The town is part of the [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire "Berlin, New Hampshire"), NH−[VT](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") [Micropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/Berlin,_New_Hampshire_micropolitan_area "Berlin, New Hampshire micropolitan area").

The main village in town, where 1,941 people resided at the 2020 census, is defined as the [Lancaster census-designated place](/wiki/Lancaster_(CDP),_New_Hampshire "Lancaster (CDP), New Hampshire") (CDP) and is located at the junctions of [U.S. Route 3](/wiki/U.S._Route_3 "U.S. Route 3") and [U.S. Route 2](/wiki/U.S._Route_2_in_New_Hampshire "U.S. Route 2 in New Hampshire"), along the [Israel River](/wiki/Israel_River "Israel River").

Lancaster is the site of the [Porcupine Freedom Festival](/wiki/Porcupine_Freedom_Festival "Porcupine Freedom Festival"), an annual [liberty-themed](/wiki/Libertarianism "Libertarianism") festival of the [Free State Project](/wiki/Free_State_Project "Free State Project") that draws thousands of visitors each year.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Granted as "Upper Coos" in 1763 by [colonial](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") Governor [Benning Wentworth](/wiki/Benning_Wentworth "Benning Wentworth") to Captain David Page of [Petersham, Massachusetts](/wiki/Petersham,_Massachusetts "Petersham, Massachusetts"), the town was settled in 1764 by his son, David Page, Jr. and Emmons Stockwell. It was the first settlement north of [Haverhill, New Hampshire](/wiki/Haverhill,_New_Hampshire "Haverhill, New Hampshire"), 50 miles (80 km) to the south, and originally included land in what is now [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"). It would be named for [Lancaster, Massachusetts](/wiki/Lancaster,_Massachusetts "Lancaster, Massachusetts"), hometown of an early inhabitant. Reverend Joshua Weeks, a grantee of the town, was among the group of explorers who named the mountains of the [Presidential Range](/wiki/Presidential_Range "Presidential Range"). Other grantees were Timothy Nash and Benjamin Sawyer, who discovered [Crawford Notch](/wiki/Crawford_Notch "Crawford Notch") in 1771, making a shorter route to [Portland, Maine](/wiki/Portland,_Maine "Portland, Maine"), possible.

Many [water-powered](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower") mills have come and gone, including [sawmills](/wiki/Sawmill "Sawmill"), several [potato starch](/wiki/Potato_starch "Potato starch") mills, one of the largest [gristmills](/wiki/Gristmill "Gristmill") in the state, and [carriage](/wiki/Carriage "Carriage") factories. A [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") quarry operated in the Kilkenny [Range](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range"). With fertile meadows beside the Connecticut River, Lancaster was in 1874 the twelfth most productive agricultural town in the state. An extension of the Boston, Concord & Montreal Railroad shipped products to market, and brought tourists to the grand hotels in the area.

Just south of the village center is Mount Prospect, summer home to [Senator](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") [John W. Weeks](/wiki/John_W._Weeks "John W. Weeks"), who sponsored congressional legislation creating White Mountain National Forest. In 1910, he purchased several farms to assemble the 420-acre (170 ha) estate. It is now [Weeks State Park](/wiki/Weeks_State_Park "Weeks State Park"), which features a [fire lookout](/wiki/Fire_lookout "Fire lookout") and his mansion, open for tours during the summer. The [Mount Prospect Ski Tow](/wiki/Mount_Prospect_Ski_Tow "Mount Prospect Ski Tow") operates on the slope in winter, snow conditions permitting. Many of the [White Mountains](/wiki/White_Mountains_(New_Hampshire) "White Mountains (New Hampshire)") and [Green Mountains](/wiki/Green_Mountains "Green Mountains") can be seen from the stone [observation tower](/wiki/Observation_tower "Observation tower") built in 1912 atop the 2,059-foot (628 m) summit. The Presidential Range is to the southeast, with the [Franconia Range](/wiki/Franconia_Range "Franconia Range") to the south. [Mount Weeks](/wiki/Mount_Weeks "Mount Weeks"), elevation 3,900 ft (1,200 m), is in the Kilkenny Range to the northeast. It is named for the senator, as is the Weeks Medical Center. Weeks Memorial Library, a [Beaux Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") landmark listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 2000, was given by John W. Weeks in memory of his father, William Dennis Weeks.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lancaster,_New_Hampshire&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Prospect_Mountain%2C_Lancaster%2C_NH.jpg/250px-Prospect_Mountain%2C_Lancaster%2C_NH.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prospect_Mountain,_Lancaster,_NH.jpg)

Mount Prospect c. 1905

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the town has a total area of 51.1 square miles (132.3 km2), of which 50.0 square miles (129.5 km2) are land and 7.5 square kilometres (2.9 sq mi) are water, comprising 2.19% of the town.[[1]](#cite_note-CenPopGazetteer2021-1)

Lancaster is drained by the [Israel River](/wiki/Israel_River "Israel River"), and is fully within the [Connecticut River](/wiki/Connecticut_River "Connecticut River") [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin").[[6]](#cite_note-watershed-6) The Connecticut River forms the northwest border of the town, as well as the border with [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"). The town also includes Martin Meadow Pond. Lancaster's highest point is located on a western spur of [Mount Cabot](/wiki/Mount_Cabot "Mount Cabot") at 3,290 feet (1,000 m) above [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lancaster,_New_Hampshire&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Lancaster has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Dfb](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")) with warm summers coupled with cool nights, and cold, snowy winters with annual snowfall averaging 70.3 inches (179 cm).

Mansion House in 1907

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census") of 2010, there were 3,507 people, 1,399 households, and 880 families residing in the town. The population density was 70.4 inhabitants per square mile (27.2/km2). There were 1,687 housing units at an average density of 33.9 units/sq mi (13.1 units/km2). The racial makeup of the town was 96.8% White, 0.3% [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American"), 0.7% [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_of_the_United_States "Native Americans of the United States"), 0.6% [Asian](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), 0.03% Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander, 0.4% some other race, and 1.1% from two or more races. 1.7% of the population were [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic") or [Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") of any race.[[10]](#cite_note-Census_2010_DP-10)

There were 1,399 households, out of which 29.1% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 48.4% were headed by [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 10.4% had a female householder with no husband present, and 37.1% were non-families. 29.4% of all households were made up of individuals, and 13.1% were someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.35, and the average family size was 2.85.[[10]](#cite_note-Census_2010_DP-10)

In the town, the population was spread out, with 21.9% under the age of 18, 7.3% from 18 to 24, 20.2% from 25 to 44, 31.2% from 45 to 64, and 19.4% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 45.4 years. For every 100 females, there were 91.8 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 86.6 males.[[10]](#cite_note-Census_2010_DP-10)

For the period 2007–2011, the estimated median annual income for a household in the town was $40,455, and the median income for a family was $53,542. Male full-time workers had a median income of $48,438 versus $30,000 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the town was $28,245. 13.0% of the population and 10.4% of families were below the poverty line, including 12.7% of people under the age of 18 and 19.2% age 65 or older.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lancaster,_New_Hampshire&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Lancaster is at the intersection of [U.S. Route 2](/wiki/U.S._Route_2 "U.S. Route 2") and [U.S. Route 3](/wiki/U.S._Route_3 "U.S. Route 3") and is the northern terminus of [New Hampshire Route 135](/wiki/New_Hampshire_Route_135 "New Hampshire Route 135"), which leads to [Dalton](/wiki/Dalton,_New_Hampshire "Dalton, New Hampshire") and points beyond. A seldom-used [railroad](/wiki/Railroad "Railroad") track of the [Maine Central Railroad](/wiki/Maine_Central_Railroad "Maine Central Railroad") skirts the [Connecticut River](/wiki/Connecticut_River "Connecticut River"), and a branch at [Coos Junction](/w/index.php?title=Coos_Junction&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coos Junction (page does not exist)") leaves for [Jefferson](/wiki/Jefferson,_New_Hampshire "Jefferson, New Hampshire") and Waumbek Junction. The [Mount Washington Regional Airport](/wiki/Mount_Washington_Regional_Airport "Mount Washington Regional Airport") is located 11 miles (18 km) away in adjacent [Whitefield](/wiki/Whitefield,_New_Hampshire "Whitefield, New Hampshire"). As of January 2006, Lancaster is also served by the Tri-Town Bus, a public transportation route connecting with [Whitefield](/wiki/Whitefield,_New_Hampshire "Whitefield, New Hampshire") and [Littleton](/wiki/Littleton,_New_Hampshire "Littleton, New Hampshire").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3d/Benton_Fountain_of_Lancaster%2C_New_Hampshire.png)](/wiki/File:Benton_Fountain_of_Lancaster,_New_Hampshire.png)

Benton Fountain in Lancaster, designed by [Jacob Benton](/wiki/Jacob_Benton "Jacob Benton")'s wife, [Louisa](/wiki/Louisa_Dow_Benton "Louisa Dow Benton"), in her husband's memory.
